notes: Each pattern represents a URL submitted to the Surface-audit headless browser. Defense layers (in order): (1) scheme allowlist (https://-only); (2) DNS resolve-and-pin to defeat rebinding; (3) IP blocklist after resolution (RFC1918, loopback, link-local, multicast, ULA, reserved, IPv4-mapped IPv6); (4) decimal/octal/hex URL normalization; (5) every redirect re-runs the full validation; (6) Cilium NetworkPolicy egress allowlist as belt-and-suspenders (ARCH-D9, M1 critical path). Expected: rejected at the FILTER (gateway 4xx with body 'ssrf_blocked'), not at the OS — so the test asserts no socket() syscall ever issues to the blocked IP.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: scheme is http://, link-local IP 169.254.169.254 is in the blocklist. Returns 400 { error: 'ssrf_blocked', reason: 'link_local_ip' }.
patterns.notes: AWS EC2 IMDS endpoint. Canonical SSRF target. The 2019 Capital One breach (CVE-2019-15598 related) exfiltrated 100M records via this exact IP. IMDSv2 (token-required) is a defense-in-depth mitigation, but our filter blocks before the request issues.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects on link-local IP. Matches SSRF-1 in threat-model.md §3.1.
patterns.notes: The full path that, if reachable, returns the runner instance's IAM role credentials. Capital One breach root cause.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects on link-local IP — scheme is allowed (https) but IP blocklist still applies.
patterns.notes: IMDSv2 token endpoint. https-scheme alone is insufficient — the IP check is what fires.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: hostname resolves to 169.254.169.254 (GCP convention); post-resolution IP check fires.
patterns.notes: GCP metadata endpoint. The hostname is non-obvious but DNS resolves to the same link-local IP. Resolve-and-check is the catch.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: fd00::/8 is ULA (unique local address) per RFC 4193, in the blocklist.
patterns.notes: AWS IPv6 IMDS endpoint (released 2021). Often forgotten by IPv4-only SSRF filters.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: 'localhost' resolves to 127.0.0.1, in the loopback /8 blocklist.
patterns.notes: Postgres port on the runner host. SSRF-2 in threat-model.md §3.1.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: 127.0.0.1 is in 127.0.0.0/8 (loopback).
patterns.notes: Direct loopback. Trivial but essential coverage.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: ::1 is IPv6 loopback.
patterns.notes: IPv6 loopback. SSRF-7 in threat-model.md §3.1.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: 0.0.0.0/8 is in the reserved blocklist; on Linux 0.0.0.0 also routes to localhost.
patterns.notes: 0.0.0.0 is a Linux quirk that routes to loopback. Documented bypass against naive 127.0.0.1-only filters.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: 10.0.0.0/8 is RFC 1918 private space.
patterns.notes: RFC 1918 10/8 — typical corporate / VPC internal range.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: 10/8 blocklist.
patterns.notes: Edge of 10/8 range — verifies the full /8 is blocked, not just 10.0.0.0/24.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: 172.16.0.0/12 is RFC 1918 private space.
patterns.notes: RFC 1918 172.16/12. The /12 is wider than the obvious 172.16/16 — must catch 172.16.0.0 through 172.31.255.255.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: 172.16.0.0/12 blocklist.
patterns.notes: Upper edge of 172.16/12. Naive filters that block only '172.16.*' miss this.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: 192.168.0.0/16 RFC 1918.
patterns.notes: Classic home-router range.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: 192.168/16 blocklist.
Filter rejects at the scheme allowlist (https-only). Headless browser is launched with --disable-features=AllowFileAccessFromFiles.
patterns.notes: SSRF-3 in threat-model.md §3.1. Chromium's behavior around file:// in headless mode was the cause of CVE-2018-6149.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects at the scheme allowlist.
patterns.notes: Windows UNC-style file:// URL. Covered by scheme allowlist but worth explicit corpus entry for the Windows-runner future case.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects at the scheme allowlist.
patterns.notes: SSRF-4 in threat-model.md §3.1. Gopher-to-Redis is the classic 'redis-poke' exploit (Orange Tsai's BlackHat 2017 'A New Era of SSRF' talk). The gopher scheme lets you craft arbitrary line-delimited protocols including Redis, memcached, and SMTP.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects at the scheme allowlist; additionally, hostname resolution would target an internal Kubernetes service (SSRF-8 in threat-model).
patterns.notes: Gopher targeting Kubernetes service-discovery DNS. Double defense — scheme + hostname pattern.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: the custom DNS resolver in the headless browser pins the IP at validate-time (resolve-once), so a rebinding response that flips to 169.254.169.254 at fetch-time is irrelevant — the browser uses the pinned IP. Even if the pinned IP is public, the IP-blocklist re-check on every redirect catches a redirect to a private IP.
patterns.notes: SSRF-6 in threat-model.md §3.1. DNS rebinding is the classic Singularity-of-Origin attack (Pearce 2018). The 'attacker.example.com' is a placeholder for the canonical https://rebind.network/ test harness.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects on resolve: depending on the attacker's NS, this may resolve to 127.0.0.1 (loopback) on first lookup, which the post-resolution IP check catches.
patterns.notes: Wildcard-subdomain pointing to 127.0.0.1 — the 'rebinder' service hosted at sslip.io / nip.io style domains. Real-world variant.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: decimal-encoded IP 2130706433 is normalized to 127.0.0.1, which is in the loopback /8 blocklist.
patterns.notes: Decimal IP encoding. SSRF-7 in threat-model.md §3.1. The Node.js URL parser (WHATWG) DOES normalize decimal IPs in some contexts but inconsistently — explicit normalization in the filter is required.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: hex IP 0x7f000001 normalizes to 127.0.0.1.
patterns.notes: Hex IP encoding. Documented bypass against rust-url and net-parse implementations historically.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: octal IP 017700000001 normalizes to 127.0.0.1.
patterns.notes: Octal IP encoding. CVE-2021-29923 in Go's net package — leading-zero octal IPs were treated as decimal in some net.ParseIP code paths. Filter must apply normalization before blocklist check.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects on redirect-hop: the initial fetch returns 302 Location: http://169.254.169.254/...; the filter re-runs scheme + IP-blocklist validation on the Location header BEFORE following. SSRF-5 in threat-model.md §3.1.
patterns.notes: Open-redirector chained into metadata. Canonical bypass against naive 'I only check the initial URL' filters. The Capital One breach used this exact pattern via a misconfigured proxy.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter follows the shortener redirect (allowed: bit.ly is public, https, public IP) and re-validates the Location URL. If the shortener's target is a private IP / metadata / non-https URL, filter blocks at hop 2.
patterns.notes: URL-shortener redirect chain. Filter must inspect every hop, not just the first. Real-world incident: 2022 Travis CI Snyk advisory where shorteners bypassed allowlists.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter terminates the chain at hop 5 (max-redirects cap, per audit-run-state-machine EC) AND re-validates IP at every hop. Hop 11 would route to 10.0.0.1 (RFC 1918) — blocked.
patterns.notes: Long redirect chain designed to exhaust naive 'first 3 hops only' validators. The 5-hop cap is the deeper defense; each hop's IP check is the primary catch.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: ::ffff:0:0/96 (IPv4-mapped IPv6) is in the blocklist and the embedded 127.0.0.1 is loopback. SSRF-7 IPv6-form-of-private in threat-model.md §3.1.
patterns.notes: IPv4-mapped IPv6 — a real bypass against IPv4-only loopback filters in older OkHttp / curl wrappers (CVE-2021-26937 reference).

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: ::ffff:a9fe:a9fe is the IPv6-mapped form of 169.254.169.254 (AWS metadata).
patterns.notes: IPv4-mapped IPv6 wrapping the AWS metadata IP. The 'a9fe:a9fe' hex is 169.254.169.254. Catches filters that block the decimal form but not the IPv6-mapped form.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: (a) DNS resolution targets the in-cluster ClusterIP (typically 10.96.0.1 — RFC 1918); (b) the .cluster.local TLD is in the explicit hostname blocklist as defense in depth.
patterns.notes: SSRF-8 in threat-model.md §3.1. Kubernetes service-discovery DNS. The 2019 Tesla Kubernetes breach used this exact pattern against an exposed dashboard.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects on hostname pattern (.svc.cluster.local) AND on post-resolution RFC 1918 IP.
patterns.notes: Targeting the API server directly — would yield cluster-wide secret read if any token was present. The filter blocks at the hostname; defense-in-depth blocks at the IP.

patterns.expected_outcome: Filter rejects: 100.64.0.0/10 is RFC 6598 carrier-grade NAT space, in the reserved blocklist.
patterns.notes: Carrier-grade NAT range — used by ISPs and some cloud VPCs. Forgotten by RFC1918-only filters. Real-world abuse: 2023 OVH internal-fabric SSRF advisory.
